coredata.dc:title: Selective and sensitive simultaneous detection of tartrazine and Hg2+ based on N-doped yellow-green fluorescent carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Lin
Jiang, Lei
Liu, Ze
Xiang, Yingying
Song, Fei
Tu, Yujiao
coredata.dc:description: Tartrazine preferred in food and textile industries, but excessive consumption threats human health. Due to toxicity, non-biodegradability, and trace accumulation in the food chains, Hg2+ has attracted people's attention. Thus, it is important to detect tartrazine and Hg2+ with sensitivity way. Using VB2 and citrate as precursors, yellow-green fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal method. The N-CDs were purified by dialysis, thin layer chromatography and chromatographic column. Excellent linear ranges of 0.25–5 μM and 5–50 μM of tartrazine were obtained with low detection limits 34.98 nM, while good linear ranges of 0.25–5 μM and 10–50 μM of Hg2+ with low detection limits 29.64 nM, respectively. In addition, the quenching mechanisms of targets to the fluorescent of N-CDs were illuminated. Furthermore, the nanoprobe was efficiently applied to determine the content of tartrazine in carbonated drink, fruit jelly and sugar samples, and Hg2+ in tap water, lake water, and river water samples. It showed great potential to detect tartrazine and Hg2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tartrazine
Hg2+
Inner filter effect
Static quenching
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity of tartrazine and Hg2+ detection
Possible quenching mechanisms of tartrazine and Hg2+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart of N-CDs preparation and purification.
(a) TEM (inset: HR-TEM) and (b) FTIR spectrum (c) XRD pattern of N-CDs.
(a) XPS full scan spectrum of the prepared crude N-CDs. High-resolution C1s spectra (b), N1s spectra (c) and O1s spectra (d).
(a) UV–vis, excitation and emission spectra, (b) Excitation and emission spectra, (c) Emission of N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths.

Fluorescence quenching efficiency of metal ions and standard solutions (a), l-amino acids, vitamins and food additives (b) to the crude N-CDs. Fluorescence quenching efficiency of food additives (c), and metal ions (d) to the secondary purified N-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs with various tartrazine (0–195 μM) (a) and Hg2+ (0–55 μM) concentrations (d). Linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency of the crude N-CDs and tartrazine concentrations (5–70 μM, 95–195 μM) (b) and Hg2+ concentrations (0–20 μM, 20–55 μM) (e). Linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency of the purified N-CDs and tartrazine (0.25–5 μM, 5–50 μM) (c) and Hg2+ concentrations (0.25–5 μM, 10–50 μM) (f).

UV–vis absorption of tartrazine, N-CDs, N-CDs + tartrazine (a) and decay lifetime of N-CDs, N-CDs + tartrazine (b). UV–vis absorption of tartrazine and Ex, Em of N-CDs (c). UV–vis absorption of Hg2+, N-CDs, N-CDs + Hg2+ (d) and decay lifetime of N-CDs, N-CDs + Hg2+ (e). UV–vis absorption of Hg2+ and Ex, Em of N-CDs (f).
Comparison of detection performance of tartrazine with CDs.
Comparison of quenching mechanism and LOD with the reported methods of Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Selective and sensitive simultaneous detection of tartrazine and Hg2+ based on N-doped yellow-green fluorescent carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Key Laboratory of Food-Safety Testing Technology, Yunnan Key Laboratory of Metal-Organic Molecular Material and Device, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Kunming University, Kunming, 650214, China

Yunnan Key Laboratory of Metal-Organic Molecular Material and Device
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Minimally Invasive Intervention, The Third Affiliated Hospital of Kunming Medical University, Kunming, 650118, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Third Affiliated Hospital of Kunming Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Minimally Invasive Intervention, the Third Affiliated Hospital of Kunming Medical University, Kunming, 650118, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tartrazine preferred in food and textile industries, but excessive consumption threats human health. Due to toxicity, non-biodegradability, and trace accumulation in the food chains, Hg2+ has attracted people's attention. Thus, it is important to detect tartrazine and Hg2+ with sensitivity way. Using VB2 and citrate as precursors, yellow-green fluorescent carbon dots were prepared by hydrothermal method. The N-CDs were purified by dialysis, thin layer chromatography and chromatographic column. Excellent linear ranges of 0.25–5 μM and 5–50 μM of tartrazine were obtained with low detection limits 34.98 nM, while good linear ranges of 0.25–5 μM and 10–50 μM of Hg2+ with low detection limits 29.64 nM, respectively. In addition, the quenching mechanisms of targets to the fluorescent of N-CDs were illuminated. Furthermore, the nanoprobe was efficiently applied to determine the content of tartrazine in carbonated drink, fruit jelly and sugar samples, and Hg2+ in tap water, lake water, and river water samples. It showed great potential to detect tartrazine and Hg2+.

Schematic of the synthetic and purification route for yellow-green fluorescent N-CDs and the applications in detection of tartrazine and Hg2+.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food dyes is usually added to the food and textile industries, and are divided into natural and synthetic dyes. Derived from the animal, plant and microbiology, the natural dyes benefit to human health. There are some advantages of synthetic dyes, such as stability, low cost, easy handling and control over the intensity of the colourant, so they are popular. Some azo dyes usually contain aromatic rings and NN bonds which can be cleaved into aromatic amine, thus lead to cancer [1,2].

As an azo synthetic dye, tartrazine (C16H9N4Na3O9S2, E102), is popularly used in food and textile industries. The satisfactory daily dietary allowance of tartrazine is 7.5 mg/kg·bw/day [3–5]. Long-term consumption of food containing excessive tartrazine may cause allergies, diarrhea, hyperactivity, and even affect the intellectual development of children [6–8]. In the metabolic process of tartrazine, the toxicity is produce though reductive transformation of the azo bond. Thus, it is important to detect tartrazine and Hg2+ with selective and sensitive method.

Heavy metal pollution is becoming more and more serious with the continuous development of industry. It seriously threaten the environment and human health [9,10]. Especially, Hg2+ has attracted people's attention based on it's toxicity, non-biodegradability and trace accumulation in the food chains [3].

Usually, typical technologies are employed for the analysis of food dyes, such as UV–vis, near-infrared (NIR), Raman spectroscopy, and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11–14]. Atomic absorption and mass spectrometry are often used for Hg2+ detection [15,16]. However, due to the disadvantages of interference [17–19], extensive, low sensitivity [20], or costly instrument and specialized operator, they do not satisfactory. Whereas fluorescence is preferred with excellent selectivity and sensitivity [21–23].

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in detecting of metal, pollution, and as drug delivery vectors [24–28]. In this work, yellow-green fluorescent CDs (N-CDs) were prepared with VB2 and citrate as precursors. Furthermore, the nanoprobe N-CDs was applied for amaranth and Hg2+ detection in real samples.

Analytical reagents, used in this work, include trypan blue (TB), new cooccine (E124), congo red (CR), tartrazine (E102), ethanol, rhodamine B (Rb), n-propanol, dimethylformamidel (DMF), CaCl2, citrate (CA), acetone, CoCl2, o-phenylenediamine (oPD), amaranth (E123), BaCl2, methanol, HgCl2, riboflavin (VB2) (E101), FeCl3, vitamin C, Pb(NO3)2, vitamin B3, CuSO4, vitamin E, AgNO3, Bi, Ni(NO3)2, KCl, MgCl2, l-lysine, l-threonine, l-tryptophan, l-isoleucine, l-proline, l-leucine, l-glycine, l-glutamine, l-histidine, l-valine, l-alanine, l-arginine, l-glutamate, l-cysteine, l-aspartic acid, l-methionine, l-tyrosine, l-phenylalanine and l-serine, were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Standard solutions fluorine, chlorine, bromine, iodine, nitrate, phosphate, nitrite, sulfate, carbonate, bicarbonate, and acetate were provided from Xiamen Haibao Technology Co., Ltd. (Xiamen, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30S, FEI, Netherlands). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (TENSOR 27, Bruker, Germany). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance). G9800A fluorospectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Spectrofluorometer (FS5, England). Vacuum freeze-drying system (CHRIST ALPHA 1–4, Germany). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) Spectrometer (Bruker AM-400).

Briefly, 1.2 g VB2 and 1.5 g citrate dissolved in 20 mL n-propanol, and transferred to the polytetrafluoroethylene high temperature reactor at 180 °C (4 h). After centrifugation (11,000 g, 10 min), the supernatant was filtered and dialyzed by dialysis tube with cut off molecular weight 1 KDa against ddH2O (48 h). The crude N-CDs with yellow-green fluorescence were obtained after freeze-drying treating, then characterized by FTIR, XPS and XRD.

By silica gel column chromatography, after eluting with gradient CH2Cl2 and MeOH (1:0 to 10:1), the primary purified N-CDs eluate showing yellow-green fluorescence were collected and concentrated under vacuum. Then, the N-CDs were submitted to silica gel column chromatography and gradient elution with petroleum ether and acetone (4:1 to 1:2), and detected with thin layer chromatography (TLC). The secondary purified N-CDs were characterize by TEM and NMR with chemical shifts in ppm rel. to Me4Si.

The preparation conditions of N-CDs were optimized, including different solvents (DMF, n-propanol, acetone, ethanol, methanol), proportions of VB2 and citrate (nVB2/nCA = 1:50, 2:50, 3:50, 4:50 and 1:5), reaction temperatures (170–220 °C), and reaction times (2–12 h). Quantum yield (QY) was measured.

The selective test of N-CDs to different metal ions, amino acids, standard solutions, vitamins and food additives was performed. Typically, 7 μL N-CDs solution was added with above materials (1 mM), then diluted to 4.0 mL with sterile H2O. Fluorescence intensity was measured after 3 min. The effect of tartrazine and Hg2+ (1 mM) towards the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was studied at different pHs to estimate the coexisting interference.

The QY value with quinine-sulfate for fluorescence measurement dissolved in H2SO4 (0.1 M) was 54% (Ex 360 nm) which was appointed as a reference [29]. The QY value as follow: Q
CDs
= Q
R
(I
CDs
/I
R
) (A
R
/A
CDs
) (η
CDs
2
/η
R
2 ).

To calculate fluorescence lifetime, two equations are used:
τ =
∑
A n
τ n 2
A n
τ n
( n = 1,2,3 … … )
Y= B + A
1 *exp(-i/τ
1 ) + … + A
n *exp(-i/τ
n ) (n = 1,2,3 …...)

To study the actual probability of N-CDs. Carbonated drink, fruit jelly, and sugar samples were chosen for tartrazine determination. Carbonated drink was sonicated at 30 min and filtered. 10 g fruit jelly was crushed, added 10 mL water, centrifuged (11,000 g, 10 min) and filtered. 10 g sugar was crushed, added 10 mL water (30 °C) and filtered. All tap water, lake water, and river water samples were filtered for Hg2+ determination. Meantime, spiking experiment as follows: 1 mM tartrazine and Hg2+ standard solution with different volumes were added to the treated samples containing 10 μL N-CDs, then the recoveries of tartrazine and Hg2+ were calculated. All filter process with 0.22 μm filter membrane.

Prepared yellow-green crude N-CDs solutions by hydrothermal method were purified with dialysis, thin layer chromatography and chromatographic column (Fig. 1 ). When the selected solvent, the proportion of VB2 and citrate, reaction temperature and reaction time were n-propanol, 2:50, 180 °C and 4 h, respectively, the QY value of N-CDs reached maximum (Fig. S1). No signals were found in the 1HNMR and 13CNMR of the secondary purified N-CDs (Fig. S2), indicating the carbon nanocapsules without molecular structure.

The average diameter of N-CDs was about 2.456 nm with uniform spherical particles, and the lattice spacing was about 0.206 nm (Fig. 2 a). The absorption peaks presented at 3,419.19 cm−1, 2,962.14 cm−1, 1,619.92 cm−1, 1,407.78 cm−1 and 1112.73 cm−1, attributing to O–H bond, the weak C–H bond, CO bond, C–H bond and C–O bond, respectively (Fig. 2b). The 2θ value was 22.05 by XRD (Fig. 2c).

The peaks presented at 284.8 eV, 400.0 eV and 532.0 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 3 a). Three peaks presented at 284.75 eV, 286.3 eV and 288.2 eV, attributing to CC, C–N and CO in C1s spectrum, respectively (Fig. 3b). In N1s spectrum, only one peak at 400.1 eV corresponding to C–N (Fig. 3c). Two peaks presented at 531.4 eV and 532.1 eV, attributing to CO/O–CO and O–H in O1s spectrum, respectively (Fig. 3d). Thus, N-CDs showed good water solubility with rich hydrophilic and amino functional groups.

A weak peak around 262 nm, corresponding to π-π* transitions (Fig. 4 a). The Em and optimal Ex of N-CDs were 531 nm and 450 nm, respectively (Fig. 4b). When Ex shifted from 390 to 450 nm, fluorescence intensity enhanced, and then lowered after Ex excessing 460. Red shift occurs, showing wavelength dependent (Fig. 4c).

N-CDs fluorescence stability was performed. pH 8.0 was the optimal pH (Fig. S3a). N-CDs was stable within 0–2.0 mM NaCl (Fig. S3b). Slightly variation of fluorescence intensity was observed after adding 0–100 mM H2O2, revealing better antioxidant performance (Fig. S3c). Strong photostability existed under natural light (Fig. S3d). Obvious decrease of fluorescence quenching efficiency within 60 min was observed, indicating instability under UV light (Fig. S3e).
The QY values of the crude and purified N-CDs were 13.55% and 25.11%, respectively, based on quinine-sulfate for fluorescence measurement (QY = 54%).

The selectivity of N-CDs were monitored. Only tartrazine and Hg2+ could quench the yellow-green fluorescence with high efficiency (Fig. 5 a and b). The N-CDs could not be used for other heavy metal detection like iron and lead (Fig. 5a). Therefore, N-CDs could be as a nanoprobe for tartrazine and Hg2+detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of tartrazine and Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pHs on N-CDs fluorescence intensity was measured within pH 2.2–12 after adding tartrazine and Hg2+. The maximum quenching efficiency was obtained at pH 10.0 with tartrazine, and at pH 7.0 with Hg2+ (Fig. S4a and Fig. 4b), respectively. 3 min was the optimal reaction time (Fig. S4c and Fig. 4d). To study the interfere with each other, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of Hg2+ and tartrazine towards N-CDs were performed at pH 7.0 and pH 10.0, respectively. There was no interfere between tartrazine and Hg2+ at different pHs (Fig. S5). Huang et al. reported that the sequential detection of Fe3+ and dopamine by Cu-Cy nanosheets with red fluorescence [30].

To enhance the luminescence properties of CDs, nitrogen-doping is the effective strategy. In this work, VB2 was used as the nitrogen dopant. After the introduction of nitrogen, the detection limit was lower [48–50]. In terms of detection ability of N-CDs towards tartrazine and Hg2+ in the field of CDs, compared with other reports, though the line range was wider with moderate LOD based on fluorescent, it possesses some advantages. It is easy and eco-friendly for synthesis without toxic chemical agents [30]. The N-CDs were purified by dialysis, thin layer chromatography and chromatographic column, and can be used as fluorescent probes to simultaneously detect tartrazine and Hg2+ without interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanisms of tartrazine and Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The quenching mechanisms of quencher to the fluorescence of CDs are mainly include five types, such as static quenching (SQ), inner filter effect (IFE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), dynamic quenching (DQ) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [51].

To study the quenching mechanism of tartrazine towards the fluorescence of N-CDs, UV–vis absorption were analyzed (Fig. 7 a). We found that there was no variation with or without tartrazine of the absorption of N-CDs. Therefore, no new substance was formed, and SQ process was not worked. Whereas, change existed with or without Hg2+ of the absorption of N-CDs. Therefore, SQ process maybe worked. (Fig. 7d).

There was almost no change without (4.71 ns) or with tartrazine (4.69 ns) based on fluorescence decay lifetime (Fig. 7b). Therefore, DQ, PET and FRET were excluded, while IFE may be work [51]. Similarly, no change of fluorescence decay lifetime was observed without (4.71 ns) or with Hg2+ (4.65 ns) (Fig. 7e), so DQ, PET and FRET were excluded, and then SQ or IFE maybe play in the quenching process of Hg2+ towards the fluorescence of N-CDs.

We could find overlap existed between N-CDs excitation spectrum and the tartrazine absorption spectrum (Fig. 7c), whereas that of Hg2+ did not (Fig. 7f). Therefore, IFE worked in tartrazine quenching the fluorescence. But for Hg2+, IFE were exclude [51].

Parker equation, as follow, was used to verify IFE [51].
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d
A
e x
1 −
10
− d
A
e x
10
g
A
e m
2.3 s
A
e m
1 −
10
− s
A
e m

F
cor
/F
obsd value enhanced from 1.079 to 1.841 when tartrazine concentrations from 0 to 25 μM (Fig. S6). The result matched with Parker equation. Hence, the mechanism of tartrazine quenching N-CDs fluorescent was IFE.

Stern-Volmer equation was used to verify SQ [51]:
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]

The slope (0.32125, 0.26104, 0.191) and Kq (6.82 × 1013 M−1 S−1, 5.54 × 1013 M−1 S−1, 4.06 × 1013 M−1 S−1) decreased when temperature (288 K, 298 K, 310 K) raised (Fig. S7), thus SQ worked. Based on τ/τ
0 = 1, the effects of temperature on Kq and UV–vis absorption, the quenching process of Hg2+ for N-CDs fluorescence was SQ. More reports on SQ dominated in Hg2+ quenching the fluorescence of CDs [38–41].

The tartrazine content in real carbonated drink, fruit jelly, and sugar samples was detect by yellow-green fluorescent nanoprobe N-CDs. The recovery rates of tartrazine fall within 97.2–104.6% (Table S1) (n = 3). Hg2+ content in tap water, lake water, and river water was also analyzed. The recovery rates of Hg2+ fall within 101.1–104.8% (Table S2) (n = 3). It showed the sensitivity of N-CDs on the content determination of tartrazine and Hg2+. The recovery rates of tartrazine with N-CDs based on fluorescent were similar to HPLC, but higher than UV–vis. However, HPLC with expensive instrument and specialized operator. Due to simple and faster preparation, N-CDs were used for tartrazine and Hg2+ detection in real samples.

The bright yellow-green fluorescent nanoprobe was prepared. Dialysis, thin layer chromatography and chromatographic column were employed for the further purification of N-CDs. It exhibited good fluorescence characters, and used for tartrazine and Hg2+ detection with excellent linear ranges and LOD. In addition, the nanoprobe could be efficiently applied to determine tartrazine in carbonated drink, fruit jelly, and sugar samples, also Hg2+ in tap water, lake water, and river water samples. Therefore, it showed a future with great potential to simultaneously detect tartrazine and Hg2+.

Lin Yuan: Designed the experiment, Writing – original draft. Lei Jiang: Designed the experiment, Writing – original draft. Ze Liu: Data curation. Yingying Xiang: Designed the experiment, Writing – original draft. Fei Song: Methodology. Yujiao Tu: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
